section.title: The Unmarried and Widows
section.sectionRationale: Paul continues his reply about marriage by turning to the unmarried, the betrothed, and widows. In view of the present distress and the shortness of the time before Christ returns, he commends singleness as a way of undivided devotion to the Lord, while insisting that marriage is no sin. He frees the widow to remarry, but only in the Lord, and judges her happier if she remains as she is. The whole passage relativizes marriage and every earthly attachment under the passing away of this world and the nearness of Christ.
section.thesis: Because the time is short and this world is passing away, singleness is a good path of undivided devotion to the Lord and Paul commends it, yet marriage is no sin; the widow is free to remarry only in the Lord, though she is happier to remain as she is, for every earthly tie is now held loosely in light of Christ.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the present distress and the freedom to marry without sin; the time is short and the world passing away; undivided devotion to the Lord; and the betrothed and the widow, free but happier as they are.

units.label: In view of the present distress

verses.text: Now about {{parthenos|virgins}}, I have no command from the Lord, but I give a judgment as one who by the Lord’s mercy is trustworthy.
Because of the present {{ananke|crisis}}, I think it is good for a man to remain as he is.
Are you committed to a wife? Do not seek to be released. Are you free of commitment? Do not look for a wife.
But if you do marry, you have not sinned. And if a virgin marries, she has not sinned. But those who marry will face troubles in this life, and I want to spare you this.
What I am saying, brothers, is that the {{kairos|time}} is short. From now on those who have wives should live as if they had none;
those who weep, as if they did not; those who are joyful, as if they were not; those who make a purchase, as if they had nothing;
and those who use the things of this world, as if not dependent on them. For this world in its present form is passing away.
I want you to be free from concern. The unmarried man is concerned about the work of the Lord, how he can please the Lord.
But the married man is concerned about the affairs of this world, how he can please his wife,

and his interests are divided. The unmarried woman or virgin is concerned about the work of the Lord, how she can be holy in both body and spirit. But the married woman is concerned about the affairs of this world, how she can please her husband.
I am saying this for your own good, not to restrict you, but in order to promote proper decorum and {{aperispastos|undivided devotion}} to the Lord.

units.label: The betrothed and the widow
units.note: free, yet happier as they are

verses.text: However, if someone thinks he is acting inappropriately toward his betrothed, and if she is beyond her youth and they ought to marry, let him do as he wishes; he is not sinning; they should get married.
But the man who is firmly established in his heart and under no constraint, with control over his will and resolve in his heart not to marry the virgin, he will do well.
So then, he who marries the virgin does well, but he who does not marry her does even better.
A wife is {{deo|bound}} to her husband as long as he lives. But if her husband dies, she is free to marry anyone she wishes, as long as he belongs to the Lord.
In my judgment, however, she is happier if she remains as she is. And I think that I too have the Spirit of God.

parthenos.gloss: A virgin, an unmarried young person. Paul addresses those who have never married, giving apostolic counsel rather than a direct command from the Lord, and commending the single life as a genuine and honorable option in view of the times.

ananke.gloss: Distress, necessity, hard pressure. The present crisis may refer to hardships already pressing on the Corinthian church, or more broadly to the tribulation of the whole age between Christ's comings. Either way it shapes Paul's counsel to hold marriage plans loosely.

kairos.gloss: An appointed time, a season, here the shortened time before the end. Paul says the time has been drawn together and is short, so that every earthly attachment, marriage, grief, joy, possessions, is to be held with an open hand in light of Christ's coming.

aperispastos.gloss: Without distraction, undividedly. The word pictures attention that is not pulled in many directions. Paul commends singleness not as superior holiness but as a life freed for undistracted, wholehearted attention to the Lord and His work.

deo.gloss: To bind, to tie. A wife is bound to her husband as long as he lives; the marriage bond is for life. When death dissolves it, the widow is free to remarry, but Paul adds the crucial limit: only in the Lord, to one who belongs to Christ.

basic.context: Paul keeps answering the Corinthians' questions, now about those who have never married and about widows. He gives his considered counsel: given the hard pressures of the present time and the shortness of the age before Christ returns, staying single is a good choice because it frees a person for undistracted devotion to the Lord. But he is careful to say that marrying is not a sin. A widow is free to remarry, but only to someone who belongs to the Lord, though Paul thinks she will be happier if she stays single.
basic.hermeneutics: Paul flags that on this question he has no direct command from Jesus, so he offers trustworthy apostolic judgment led by the Spirit. His counsel is shaped by circumstances, the present distress and the short time, so it is wisdom for living, not a law binding every believer to singleness. Marriage and singleness are both honored; the deeper call is undivided devotion to Christ.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul writes about the parthenos, the virgin or unmarried. His counsel is shaped by the present ananke, distress or pressure, and by the fact that the kairos, the appointed time, is short. He commends singleness as a life of aperispastos devotion, undistracted attention to the Lord. And he teaches that a wife is deo, bound, to her husband for life, freed to remarry only in the Lord when he dies.
basic.typology: The passing away of this world in its present form points forward to the new creation, when the shadow of marriage gives way to the reality it pictured, the marriage of the Lamb. Singleness lived for the Lord is a sign of that coming age, when believers neither marry nor are given in marriage but belong wholly to Christ.
basic.christ: Everything here is oriented to Christ: the shortness of the time is measured by His coming, undivided devotion is devotion to the Lord Jesus, and remarriage is to be only in the Lord. Singleness and marriage alike find their meaning in belonging to and pleasing Christ above every earthly tie.
basic.god: God gives some the gift of singleness and calls His people to hold this passing world loosely. He is worthy of undivided devotion, and He is honored whether one marries or remains single, so long as the heart is set on Him. Paul closes claiming the Spirit of God stands behind his counsel.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 19 records Jesus' words that some renounce marriage for the kingdom. Matthew 22 teaches that in the resurrection people neither marry nor are given in marriage. Romans 13 urges holy living because the night is nearly over. 1 Corinthians 7 verse 7 already named singleness a gift.
basic.oneStory: The whole Bible moves toward the marriage of Christ and His church, when this present world in its current form has passed away. Paul teaches the Corinthians to live now in light of that day: to marry or stay single as God gives grace, but in either case to hold this fading world loosely and give the Lord undivided devotion.

context.literary: This is the second half of Paul's teaching on marriage, still under the now about heading that answers the Corinthians' letter. It alternates between concrete counsel to virgins, the betrothed, and widows, and sweeping statements about the shortness of the time and the passing of the world. The heart of the section is verses 32 to 35, undivided devotion to the Lord, framed by pastoral cautions that marriage is no sin.
context.historical: The Corinthians faced real pressures, and the early church lived in vivid expectation of Christ's return. Some may have overvalued marriage, others despised it. Paul, himself single, commends the freedom of the unmarried life for gospel service, while resisting any teaching that marriage is spiritually inferior or forbidden.
context.canonical: Paul's counsel stands alongside Jesus' teaching that some are eunuchs for the sake of the kingdom, and that in the resurrection there is no marriage. It develops the New Testament theme of living as those on whom the end of the ages has come, holding the present world loosely because it is passing away.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To commend singleness as a good and freeing path of undivided devotion to the Lord in view of the present distress and the short time, while safeguarding the truth that marriage is no sin and honoring both callings.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Paul explicitly gives judgment, not command; his counsel to remain single is wise advice shaped by the times, not a rule binding all believers. The abiding truths are that marriage is not sin, that the world is passing away, that the widow may remarry only in the Lord, and that undivided devotion to Christ is the goal for all.

debates.question: Is the present distress a local crisis or the tribulation of the whole present age?

views.label: A specific hardship at Corinth
views.case: Paul may have in mind famine, persecution, or social upheaval pressing on the church, making marriage and family especially burdensome in that moment.

views.label: The eschatological pressure of the last days
views.case: Coupled with the time is short and the world is passing away, the distress may name the tribulation of the whole age between Christ's first and second coming.

debates.bearing: On either view Paul's counsel is wisdom for holding earthly ties loosely; it does not forbid marriage but reorients every attachment around Christ's coming.

debates.question: Do verses 36 to 38 concern a father and his daughter, or a man and his betrothed?

views.label: A man and the woman he is engaged to
views.case: The language of acting inappropriately and being under constraint fits an engaged couple deciding whether to marry, which most modern translations, including the BSB, adopt.

views.label: A father deciding about his unmarried daughter
views.case: An older reading takes the man as a father with authority to give or keep his virgin daughter in marriage, reflecting ancient family custom.

debates.bearing: Either way Paul's point holds: to marry in this case is good and no sin, and to refrain, where one is free and settled, is also good; the counsel honors both choices.

study.originalLanguages: Paul addresses the parthenos, the unmarried, with Spirit-led judgment rather than a dominical command. His advice is framed by the present ananke, the distress or pressure of the times, and by the truth that the kairos, the appointed time, has been shortened, so that even those with wives are to live as though holding this world loosely, for its present form is passing away. His aim is aperispastos devotion, undistracted attention to the Lord, freed from divided concern. And he teaches that a wife is deo, bound, to her husband for life, released to remarry only in the Lord when death dissolves the bond.

typology.type: The passing away of this world's present form
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the new creation, where the present order gives way to the age to come and the reality that earthly marriage only foreshadowed.

typology.type: Singleness for the sake of the Lord
typology.fulfillment: A living sign of the resurrection age, when believers neither marry nor are given in marriage but belong wholly to Christ the Bridegroom.

study.christ: Christ is the horizon of the whole passage. The time is short because He is coming; the world is passing away because His kingdom is dawning. Undivided devotion is devotion to the Lord Jesus, and even remarriage must be only in the Lord, to one who belongs to Him. Whether single or married, the believer's supreme concern is to please Christ and to be found wholly His when this present age gives way to the age to come.
study.god: God grants the gift of singleness to some and calls all His people to hold this fading world with an open hand. He is worthy of undivided devotion and is glorified in both the married and the unmarried who live for Him. Paul ends by claiming the Spirit of God behind his counsel, reminding the church that this wisdom comes from God, not mere human preference.

crossReferences.note: Jesus teaches that some renounce marriage for the sake of the kingdom of heaven, the freedom Paul commends here.
In the resurrection people neither marry nor are given in marriage, the age that singleness for the Lord signifies.
Live holy lives because the night is far gone and the day is near, matching the time is short.
Singleness is named a gift of grace, the foundation for Paul's counsel to the unmarried.
The marriage of the Lamb, the reality toward which this passing world and its marriages point.

study.oneStory: The Bible's story runs toward the day when this world in its present form passes away and Christ is joined forever to His bride. Living between the ages, the Corinthians are told to hold marriage, grief, joy, and possessions loosely, and to give the Lord undivided devotion. Singleness and marriage alike become ways of waiting faithfully for the Bridegroom, whose coming makes the time short and every earthly tie provisional.

quiz.q: What kind of authority does Paul claim for his counsel about virgins?

options.text: Trustworthy apostolic judgment led by the Spirit, not a direct command from the Lord.
options.note: Verse 25 says he has no command from the Lord but gives a trustworthy judgment.

options.text: A binding command from Jesus that all must obey.
options.note: Paul explicitly says he has no command from the Lord on this.

options.text: Mere personal opinion with no spiritual weight.
options.note: He claims to be trustworthy by the Lord's mercy and to have the Spirit of God.

options.text: A rule drawn only from Corinthian custom.
options.note: His counsel is shaped by the times, but it is Spirit-led apostolic judgment, not local custom.

quiz.q: Does Paul teach that it is sinful to marry?

options.text: No; if you marry you have not sinned, though married life brings added troubles.
options.note: Verse 28 plainly states that marrying is not a sin.

options.text: Yes; marriage is a lower, sinful compromise.
options.note: Paul directly denies this: you have not sinned.

options.text: Only for widows is remarriage sinful.
options.note: The widow is free to remarry in the Lord; it is not called sin.

options.text: Marrying is sinful only during a crisis.
options.note: The crisis makes marriage harder, but Paul still says marrying is no sin.

quiz.q: Why does Paul commend the single life in this passage?

options.text: It frees a person for undivided, undistracted devotion to the Lord.
options.note: Verses 32 to 35 present singleness as a life of undivided devotion to Christ.

options.text: Because marriage is impure and defiling.
options.note: Paul never calls marriage impure; he calls it no sin.

options.text: Because single people are holier than married people.
options.note: The point is undivided attention, not superior holiness.

options.text: Because the church forbids marriage.
options.note: No such ban exists; Paul upholds the freedom to marry.

quiz.q: What does Paul mean that the time is short and the world is passing away?

options.text: Every earthly attachment is to be held loosely in light of Christ's coming.
options.note: Verses 29 to 31 relativize marriage, grief, joy, and possessions under the passing world.

options.text: Believers should abandon all responsibilities immediately.
options.note: Paul does not urge neglect; he urges holding this world with an open hand.

options.text: The physical world is evil and should be despised.
options.note: Paul does not call the world evil, but passing in its present form.

options.text: Nothing we do now has any value.
options.note: The opposite; how we live now matters greatly in view of the coming age.

quiz.q: What limit does Paul place on a widow's freedom to remarry?

options.text: She may remarry, but only to someone who belongs to the Lord.
options.note: Verse 39 frees her to remarry only in the Lord.

options.text: She may never remarry under any circumstances.
options.note: Paul says she is free to remarry when her husband dies.

options.text: She may marry anyone at all with no restriction.
options.note: There is a restriction: the man must belong to the Lord.

options.text: She must remarry as quickly as possible.
options.note: Paul judges she is happier if she remains as she is.

questions: Paul commends singleness as freedom for undivided devotion to the Lord. If you are single, how might you embrace this season as an opportunity rather than only a lack?
The time is short and this world is passing away. Which earthly attachment are you gripping too tightly, and what would it look like to hold it with an open hand?
Paul wants believers to have undivided devotion to the Lord. What in your life most divides your attention from Christ, and how might you simplify?
Marriage is no sin, and singleness is a gift; both are honored. How can you resist quietly ranking one above the other, in your own heart or toward others?
A widow is free to remarry only in the Lord. How does the principle of belonging to the Lord shape the relationships and commitments you pursue?

translationNotes.note: The present crisis renders a word for distress or hard pressure; it may point to a specific hardship at Corinth or to the tribulation of the whole present age, and it shapes Paul's counsel to hold marriage plans loosely.

The BSB renders this of a man and his betrothed; an older tradition read it of a father and his unmarried daughter. Either way, to marry is good and no sin, and to refrain, where one is free, is also good.
She is free to marry anyone she wishes, as long as he belongs to the Lord, restricts remarriage to a fellow believer, only in the Lord, not to just any partner.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Corinthians 7:25-40; any em dashes rendered as commas per the no-dash style rule, with wording unchanged.
